text: Radioimmunotherapy (RIT) has emerged as a promising treatment modality for a wide variety of cancers. However, the current RIT approach faces significant challenges such as limited tumor delivery, long circulation half-life of mAbs and/or their fragments that allow non-specific binding to normal organs and immunogenicity of mAb-derived products. A better therapeutic approach would involve short circulation half-life with rapid and specific accumulation in cancer. Antibody fragments and peptides have a rapid blood clearance and fast accumulation to the target sites with an exquisite specificity to the target (low accumulation in normal tissues except the kidneys). Nanobodies are the smallest (MW < 15 kDa) functional antigen-binding fragments that are derived from heavy chain-only camelid antibodies. We investigate here the role of the amino acid tag (tag) at the C-terminus of the heavy chain of the nanobodies. Our investigations involve three nanobody preparations of 177Lu-DTPA-2Rs15d (designated as His-tag, Myc-His-tag or untagged) that differ by only a short amino acid tag and showed that the extent of accumulation in the kidneys was determined by the amino acid composition of the tag of the nanobodies. Accordingly, dynamic planar imaging of Wistar rats with 111In-DTPA-untagged-nanobodies showed a 70% decrease in kidney accumulation compared with 111In-DTPA-Myc-His-tagged-nanobodies at 50 min post injection (p.i) and a further 20% reduction at 120 min p.i.. Coinfusion of untagged nanobodies with the plasma expander Gelof Gelofusin resulted in a further 20% reduction at 120 min p.i (a 90% decrease compared with the accumulation in Myc-His-tagged nanobodies at 50 min p.i). Similar findings were observed using different 177Lu-DTPA-2Rs15d nanobody preparations in HER2pos (human epidermal growth factor receptor 2) tumor xenografted mice at 1 h post injection (p.i). Kidney accumulation was 88% lower when using Myc-His-tagged nanobodies compared with the untagged nanobodies; a further 87% decrease was observed with Gelofusin-coinfused untagged nanobodies without affecting any of the desired tumor accumulation. We demonstrate therefore a generic method to minimize the accumulation of radiolabeled nanobodies in the kidneys, resulting in a dose of 0.90 Gy/MBq to be delivered to both the tumor and the kidneys with extremely low absorption doses to normal tissues. Such a method enhances the therapeutic efficacy of the therapy. A comparative dosimetry study using 177Lu-DTPA-Trastuzumab supplied 6 times more dose to the tumor compared with 177Lu-DTPA-untagged 2Rs15d while the radioactivity burden in other organs such as lung, liver, spleen, bone and blood is 155, 34, 80, 26 and 4180 fold higher. Finally, targeted radionuclide therapy using nanobodies in mice bearing small established HER2pos tumors demonstrated a complete inhibition of tumor growth and a highly significant difference in event-free survival between the treated and control groups (P<0.0001) without any signs of renal inflammation, apoptosis or necrosis as confirmed from the histological analyses. In summary, our data demonstrated a predominant role of the C-terminus of the amino acid tag on the accumulation of the nanobodies in the kidneys of animals. The radiolabeled nanobodies demonstrated specific accumulation of cancer tissues and their successful application for the treatment of small established xenografted HER2pos tumors and highlighting thus the potential of radiolabeled nanobodies as an adjuvant therapy for the minimal residual or metastatic cancer.
